description: This figure presents the binary Fe–Be phase diagram calculated under thermodynamic equilibrium conditions, illustrating the relationship between composition (molar ratio of Fe/(Be+Fe)) and temperature. The horizontal axis spans from pure Be (0) to pure Fe (1), while the vertical axis represents temperature from 0 to 1800 °C. The diagram identifies multiple phase regions including Liquid, body-centered cubic BCC-A2 (δ-ferrite), hexagonal close-packed HCP-A3 (Be-rich terminal solid solution), and face-centered cubic FCC-A1 (γ-austenite). Notably, three intermetallic compounds are marked: FeBe₁₂ (Be-rich), FeBe₅, and FeBe₂ (Fe-rich). The diagram reveals extremely limited solid solubility of Be in austenite (<1 mol% Fe), with Be preferentially forming intermetallic compounds rather than dissolving in the FCC matrix. Conversely, the BCC-A2 ferrite phase exhibits significantly higher Be solubility. This thermodynamic data supports the experimental observation that excess Be in Fe–Mn–Al–C–Be steels precipitates as brittle Be–Fe compounds within the austenite matrix or at grain boundaries, while partial Be dissolution occurs in ferrite when present.
